title: Der singende Knochen
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 28. Text from Wikisource (twice proofread against the original). 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: The twenty-eighth tale in the Grimms’ collection (KHM 28), a short and grim little ballad of a tale. Two poor brothers hunt a land-ravaging boar; the innocent younger brother kills it with a spear given by a mysterious little man, and his envious elder brother murders him for the glory, buries him under a bridge, and wins the king’s daughter — until, years later, a shepherd carves a mouthpiece from a bone in the sand, and the bone sings out the murder of its own accord. The singing bone convicts the killer, who is sewn into a sack and drowned. Spelling follows the 1857 edition, so you will meet older forms such as gieng, fieng, that (for tat), tödtete, Thier, todt, Muth and läugnen.

sentences.t: Es war einmal in einem Lande große Klage über ein Wildschwein, das den Bauern die Äcker umwühlte, das Vieh tödtete und den Menschen mit seinen Hauern den Leib aufriß.
sentences.l: There was once in a land great lamentation over a wild boar, that rooted up the farmers’ fields, killed the cattle, and tore open people’s bodies with its tusks.
sentences.i: Once upon a time there was great lamentation in a certain land over a wild boar that churned up the farmers’ fields, killed their cattle, and ripped open people’s bodies with its tusks.
sentences.n: „aufriß“ is the preterite of aufreißen (‘to tear open’; modern aufriss), a separable verb held together at the clause end. „tödtete“ carries the old -d- spelling of töten. „den Bauern die Äcker“ uses a dative of possession: it churned up the fields belonging to the farmers.

sentences.t: Der König versprach einem jeden, der das Land von dieser Plage befreien würde, eine große Belohnung: aber das Thier war so groß und stark, daß sich niemand in die Nähe des Waldes wagte, worin es hauste.
sentences.l: The king promised to every one who would free the land from this plague a great reward: but the beast was so big and strong that no one dared venture into the neighborhood of the forest in which it dwelt.
sentences.i: The king promised a great reward to anyone who would rid the land of this scourge; but the beast was so large and strong that no one dared go near the forest where it made its lair.
sentences.n: „sich wagen“ (reflexive) = to dare to venture; „sich in die Nähe wagen“ = to dare go near. „worin“ = ‘wherein, in which’ (wo + in). „Thier“ keeps the old -h- spelling of Tier.

sentences.t: Endlich ließ der König bekannt machen wer das Wildschwein einfange oder tödte solle seine einzige Tochter zur Gemahlin haben.
sentences.l: At last the king had it made known: whoever caught or killed the wild boar should have his only daughter as wife.
sentences.i: At last the king had it proclaimed that whoever should catch or kill the wild boar should have his only daughter to wife.
sentences.n: „ließ bekannt machen“ = had it made known, proclaimed (lassen + infinitive for causing something to be done). „einfange“ and „tödte“ stand in subjunctive I of the reported proclamation. „zur Gemahlin haben“ = to have as wife (Gemahlin, an elevated word for spouse).

sentences.t: Nun lebten zwei Brüder in dem Lande, Söhne eines armen Mannes, die meldeten sich und wollten das Wagnis übernehmen.
sentences.l: Now there lived two brothers in the land, sons of a poor man, who came forward and wanted to take on the venture.
sentences.i: Now there lived in that land two brothers, the sons of a poor man, who came forward and were willing to undertake the venture.
sentences.n: „sich melden“ = to come forward, present oneself, volunteer. „die meldeten sich“ — the demonstrative die (‘they, these’) resumes the brothers, a common Grimm construction. „Wagnis“ = a hazardous undertaking (from wagen, to dare).

sentences.t: Der älteste, der listig und klug war, that es aus Hochmuth, der jüngste, der unschuldig und dumm war, aus gutem Herzen.
sentences.l: The eldest, who was cunning and clever, did it out of pride; the youngest, who was innocent and simple, out of a good heart.
sentences.i: The eldest, who was cunning and shrewd, did it out of arrogance; the youngest, who was innocent and simple, out of goodness of heart.
sentences.n: „that“ is the 1857 spelling of tat (preterite of thun = tun, ‘to do’); the silent -h- recurs throughout this edition. „dumm“ here is ‘simple, guileless’ rather than merely ‘stupid.’ Note the paired „aus Hochmuth … aus gutem Herzen“ — ‘out of pride … out of a good heart.’

sentences.t: Der König sagte „damit ihr desto sicherer das Thier findet, so sollt ihr von entgegengesetzten Seiten in den Wald gehen.“
sentences.l: The king said, “so that you the more surely find the beast, you shall go into the forest from opposite sides.”
sentences.i: The king said, “So that you may find the beast the more surely, you shall enter the forest from opposite sides.”
sentences.n: „damit … desto sicherer“ — damit is ‘so that’ (purpose); „desto + comparative“ = ‘the (more) …,’ so ‘the more surely.’ The „so“ after the clause simply resumes the main clause, common in older German.

sentences.t: Da gieng der älteste von Abend und der jüngste von Morgen hinein.
sentences.l: Then the eldest went in from evening and the youngest from morning.
sentences.i: So the eldest went in from the west and the youngest from the east.
sentences.n: „von Abend … von Morgen“: Abend (‘evening’) is an old word for the west, Morgen (‘morning’) for the east — the sun’s setting and rising directions. „gieng“ is the 1857 spelling of ging. „hinein“ (‘in, inward’) governs both brothers.

sentences.t: Und als der jüngste ein Weilchen gegangen war, so trat ein kleines Männlein zu ihm: das hielt einen schwarzen Spieß in der Hand und sprach „diesen Spieß gebe ich dir, weil dein Herz unschuldig und gut ist: damit kannst du getrost auf das wilde Schwein eingehen, es wird dir keinen Schaden zufügen.“
sentences.l: And when the youngest had gone a little while, there stepped a little man up to him: this one held a black spear in his hand and spoke, “this spear I give you, because your heart is innocent and good: with it you can go confidently against the wild boar, it will do you no harm.”
sentences.i: And when the youngest had walked a little while, a little man stepped up to him; he held a black spear in his hand and said, “This spear I give you, because your heart is innocent and good: with it you may go boldly against the wild boar, and it will do you no harm.”
sentences.n: „eingehen auf“ here has the old sense ‘to go in against, engage’ the boar. „getrost“ = confidently, with good courage. „zufügen“ (separable, zu- to the end) = to inflict; „Schaden zufügen“ = to do harm. „Männlein“ is the diminutive of Mann — the little helper is a fairy-tale fixture.

sentences.t: Er dankte dem Männlein, nahm den Spieß auf die Schulter und gieng ohne Furcht weiter.
sentences.l: He thanked the little man, took the spear on his shoulder, and went on without fear.
sentences.i: He thanked the little man, shouldered the spear, and went on without fear.
sentences.n: „dankte dem Männlein“ — danken takes the dative (‘thanked the little man’). „gieng … weiter“ (weitergehen, to go on) is separable.

sentences.t: Nicht lange so erblickte er das Thier, das auf ihn los rannte, er hielt ihm aber den Spieß entgegen, und in seiner blinden Wuth rannte es so gewaltig hinein, daß ihm das Herz entzwei geschnitten ward.
sentences.l: Not long, and he caught sight of the beast, which ran straight at him; but he held the spear out toward it, and in its blind fury it ran in so mightily that its heart was cut in two.
sentences.i: Before long he caught sight of the beast, which came charging at him; but he held out the spear to meet it, and in its blind rage it ran onto it so violently that its heart was cut in two.
sentences.n: „hielt … entgegen“ (entgegenhalten, to hold out against) is separable; „ihm … entgegen“ = ‘toward it.’ „los rannte“ (losrennen) = to run straight/off at. „entzwei“ = in two, asunder. „ward“ is the archaic preterite of werden, forming the passive ‘was cut.’

sentences.t: Da nahm er das Ungestüm auf die Schulter, gieng heimwärts und wollte es dem Könige bringen.
sentences.l: Then he took the raging thing on his shoulder, went homeward, and wanted to bring it to the king.
sentences.i: Then he took the great beast up on his shoulder, went homeward, and meant to bring it to the king.
sentences.n: „das Ungestüm“ ordinarily means ‘impetuosity, violence’; here it is used concretely of the fierce boar itself — ‘the raging thing.’ „Könige“ carries the old dative -e ending. „heimwärts“ = homeward (-wärts forms directions).

sentences.t: Als er auf der andern Seite des Waldes heraus kam, stand da am Eingang ein Haus, wo die Leute sich mit Tanz und Wein lustig machten.
sentences.l: When he came out on the other side of the forest, there stood at the entrance a house, where the people made themselves merry with dance and wine.
sentences.i: When he came out on the other side of the forest, there stood at its edge a house, where the folk were making merry with dancing and wine.
sentences.n: „kam … heraus“ (herauskommen, to come out) is separable. „sich lustig machen“ = to make merry, enjoy oneself. „am“ = an dem; „Eingang“ here is the entrance / edge of the wood.

sentences.t: Sein ältester Bruder war da eingetreten und hatte gedacht das Schwein liefe ihm doch nicht fort, erst wollte er sich einen rechten Muth trinken.
sentences.l: His eldest brother had entered there and had thought the boar would not run away from him anyhow; first he wanted to drink himself a proper courage.
sentences.i: His eldest brother had gone in there, thinking the boar would not run away from him in any case; first he wanted to drink himself a good dose of courage.
sentences.n: „eingetreten“ is the past participle of eintreten (‘to step in, enter’; separable ein-). „liefe … fort“ is subjunctive II (fortlaufen, to run away) in reported thought — ‘would run away.’ „sich Muth trinken“ = to drink oneself courage; „Muth“ is the old spelling of Mut.

sentences.t: Als er nun den jüngsten erblickte, der mit seiner Beute beladen aus dem Wald kam, so ließ ihm sein neidisches und boshaftes Herz keine Ruhe.
sentences.l: When he now caught sight of the youngest, who came out of the forest laden with his booty, his envious and malicious heart let him have no peace.
sentences.i: Now when he caught sight of the youngest coming out of the forest laden with his prey, his envious and spiteful heart would give him no peace.
sentences.n: „beladen mit“ = laden with. „Beute“ = booty, prey, spoils. „ließ ihm … keine Ruhe“ — jemandem keine Ruhe lassen = to give someone no peace (dative ihm).

sentences.t: Er rief ihm zu „komm doch herein, lieber Bruder, ruhe dich aus und stärke dich mit einem Becher Wein.“
sentences.l: He called out to him, “do come in, dear brother, rest yourself and strengthen yourself with a beaker of wine.”
sentences.i: He called out to him, “Do come in, dear brother, rest yourself and refresh yourself with a cup of wine.”
sentences.n: „rief … zu“ (zurufen, to call out to) is separable and takes the dative (ihm). „ruhe dich aus“ (sich ausruhen, to rest) is a separable reflexive; „stärke dich“ (sich stärken) = to fortify/refresh oneself. „doch“ softens the invitation (‘do come’).

sentences.t: Der jüngste, der nichts arges dahinter vermuthete, gieng hinein und erzählte ihm von dem guten Männlein, das ihm einen Spieß gegeben, womit er das Schwein getödtet hätte.
sentences.l: The youngest, who suspected nothing bad behind it, went in and told him of the good little man who had given him a spear, with which he had killed the boar.
sentences.i: The youngest, suspecting no evil behind it, went in and told him of the good little man who had given him a spear, with which he had killed the boar.
sentences.n: „nichts arges“ = nothing bad/evil (arg, ‘wicked’). „vermuthete“ is the 1857 spelling of vermutete (vermuten, to suspect). „womit“ = ‘with which’ (wo + mit). „gegeben … getödtet hätte“ — the perfect is trimmed to „gegeben“ (auxiliary dropped) and the reported „hätte getödtet“ stands in subjunctive II.

sentences.t: Der älteste hielt ihn bis zum Abend zurück, da giengen sie zusammen fort.
sentences.l: The eldest held him back until evening; then they went off together.
sentences.i: The eldest kept him back until evening, and then they set off together.
sentences.n: „hielt … zurück“ (zurückhalten, to hold back) and „giengen … fort“ (fortgehen, to go off) are both separable. „giengen“ is the 1857 plural of gingen.

sentences.t: Als sie aber in der Dunkelheit zu der Brücke über einen Bach kamen, ließ der älteste den jüngsten vorangehen, und als er mitten über dem Wasser war, gab er ihm von hinten einen Schlag, daß er todt hinabstürzte.
sentences.l: But when they came in the darkness to the bridge over a brook, the eldest let the youngest go ahead, and when he was in the middle over the water, he gave him from behind a blow, so that he plunged down dead.
sentences.i: But when they came in the dark to the bridge over a brook, the eldest let the youngest go ahead, and when he was midway over the water, he struck him a blow from behind, so that he fell down dead.
sentences.n: „ließ … vorangehen“ — lassen + infinitive (‘let him go ahead’); vorangehen is separable. „todt“ is the 1857 spelling of tot (‘dead’). „hinabstürzte“ (hinabstürzen, to plunge down) is separable. This is the murder at the heart of the tale — told bluntly, and not softened.

sentences.t: Er begrub ihn unter der Brücke, nahm dann das Schwein und brachte es dem König mit dem Vorgeben er hätte es getödtet; worauf er die Tochter des Königs zur Gemahlin erhielt.
sentences.l: He buried him under the bridge, then took the boar and brought it to the king with the pretense that he had killed it; whereupon he received the king’s daughter as wife.
sentences.i: He buried him under the bridge, then took the boar and brought it to the king with the false claim that he himself had killed it; whereupon he received the king’s daughter to wife.
sentences.n: „begrub“ is the preterite of begraben (‘to bury’). „mit dem Vorgeben“ = under the pretense; „hätte getödtet“ is subjunctive II of the reported claim. „worauf“ = ‘whereupon.’ „erhielt“ (erhalten) = received, obtained.

sentences.t: Als der jüngste Bruder nicht wieder kommen wollte, sagte er „das Schwein wird ihm den Leib aufgerissen haben,“ und das glaubte jedermann.
sentences.l: When the youngest brother would not come back again, he said, “the boar will have torn open his body,” and that everyone believed.
sentences.i: When the youngest brother did not come back, he said, “The boar must have ripped his body open,” and everyone believed it.
sentences.n: „wird … aufgerissen haben“ is the future perfect used to voice a supposition — ‘will (surely) have torn open,’ i.e. ‘must have.’ „aufgerissen“ is the past participle of aufreißen. „das glaubte jedermann“ — the object das is fronted for emphasis (‘that everyone believed’).

sentences.t: Weil aber vor Gott nichts verborgen bleibt, sollte auch diese schwarze That ans Licht kommen.
sentences.l: But because before God nothing stays hidden, this black deed too was to come to light.
sentences.i: But because nothing stays hidden before God, this black deed too was destined to come to light.
sentences.n: „verborgen“ is the past participle of verbergen (‘to hide, conceal’). „sollte … kommen“ here carries the sense of destiny — ‘was to / was destined to come.’ „That“ is the 1857 spelling of Tat (‘deed’); „ans“ = an das.

sentences.t: Nach langen Jahren trieb ein Hirt einmal seine Herde über die Brücke und sah unten im Sande ein schneeweißes Knöchlein liegen und dachte das gäbe ein gutes Mundstück.
sentences.l: After long years a shepherd once drove his herd over the bridge and saw below in the sand a snow-white little-bone lying, and thought that would make a good mouthpiece.
sentences.i: After long years a shepherd once drove his flock across the bridge, and saw a snow-white little bone lying below in the sand, and thought it would make a good mouthpiece.
sentences.n: „trieb“ is the preterite of treiben (‘to drive [a herd]’). „gäbe“ is subjunctive II of geben in the idiom „das gäbe“ = ‘that would make / yield.’ „Knöchlein“ (and the song’s „Knöchelein“) is the diminutive of Knochen — the singing bone that gives the tale its name.

sentences.t: Da stieg er herab, hob es auf und schnitzte ein Mundstück daraus für sein Horn.
sentences.l: So he climbed down, picked it up, and carved a mouthpiece out of it for his horn.
sentences.i: So he climbed down, picked it up, and carved a mouthpiece from it for his horn.
sentences.n: „stieg … herab“ (herabsteigen, to climb down) and „hob … auf“ (aufheben, to pick up) are both separable. „schnitzen“ = to carve, whittle. „daraus“ = ‘out of it’ (da + aus).

sentences.t: Als er zum erstenmal darauf geblasen hatte, so fieng das Knöchlein zu großer Verwunderung des Hirten von selbst an zu singen
sentences.l: When he had blown on it for the first time, the little bone, to the great astonishment of the shepherd, began of itself to sing
sentences.i: When he had blown on it for the first time, the little bone, to the shepherd’s great astonishment, began of its own accord to sing:
sentences.n: „fieng … an“ (anfangen, to begin) is the 1857 spelling of fing an — separable, the an going to the clause end. „von selbst“ = of its own accord. „zu großer Verwunderung“ = ‘to (the) great astonishment.’ This is the singing-bone motif: the murdered innocent’s remains speak out and accuse the killer — a very old and widespread folk theme.

sentences.t:
„Ach, du liebes Hirtelein,
du bläst auf meinem Knöchelein,
mein Bruder hat mich erschlagen,
unter der Brücke begraben,
um das wilde Schwein,
für des Königs Töchterlein.“
sentences.i:
“Ah, you dear little shepherd,
you blow upon my little bone;
it was my brother who slew me,
and buried me under the bridge,
all for the sake of the wild boar,
and for the king’s own daughter.”
sentences.n: The bone’s song, in simple rhymed couplets (Hirtelein/Knöchelein, erschlagen/begraben, Schwein/Töchterlein). The diminutives Hirtelein, Knöchelein, Töchterlein (‘little shepherd, little bone, little daughter’) give it a plaintive, nursery-rhyme tenderness. „um … für“ = ‘for the sake of … for’ — the motive laid bare. „erschlagen“ = slain (past participle of erschlagen).

sentences.t: „Was für ein wunderliches Hörnchen,“ sagte der Hirt, „das von selber singt, das muß ich dem Herrn König bringen.“
sentences.l: “What a curious little horn,” said the shepherd, “that sings by itself; that I must bring to the lord king.”
sentences.i: “What a strange little horn,” said the shepherd, “that sings all by itself — this I must bring to my lord the king.”
sentences.n: „was für ein“ = ‘what a / what kind of a.’ „wunderlich“ = strange, curious, odd. „von selber“ = by itself (a variant of von selbst). „dem Herrn König“ — Herr as a title of respect, ‘the lord king’; both in the dative after bringen.

sentences.t: Als er damit vor den König kam, fieng das Hörnchen abermals an sein Liedchen zu singen.
sentences.l: When he came with it before the king, the little horn began once more to sing its little song.
sentences.i: When he came before the king with it, the little horn began once more to sing its little song.
sentences.n: „fieng … an“ (anfangen), separable, 1857 spelling. „abermals“ = once more, again (elevated). „Liedchen“ is the diminutive of Lied (‘song’).

sentences.t: Der König verstand es wohl, und ließ die Erde unter der Brücke aufgraben, da kam das ganze Gerippe des Erschlagenen zum Vorschein.
sentences.l: The king understood it well, and had the earth dug up under the bridge; there the whole skeleton of the slain man came to light.
sentences.i: The king understood it well, and had the earth dug up beneath the bridge; whereupon the whole skeleton of the slain man came to light.
sentences.n: „ließ … aufgraben“ — lassen + infinitive (‘had it dug up’); aufgraben is separable. „zum Vorschein kommen“ is a set phrase = to come to light, appear. „des Erschlagenen“ = ‘of the slain man,’ the past participle of erschlagen used as a noun.

sentences.t: Der böse Bruder konnte die That nicht läugnen, ward in einen Sack genäht und lebendig ersäuft, die Gebeine des Gemordeten aber wurden auf den Kirchhof in ein schönes Grab zur Ruhe gelegt.
sentences.l: The wicked brother could not deny the deed, was sewn into a sack and drowned alive; the bones of the murdered man, however, were laid to rest in the churchyard in a fine grave.
sentences.i: The wicked brother could not deny the deed; he was sewn into a sack and drowned alive, while the bones of the murdered man were laid to rest in a fine grave in the churchyard.
sentences.n: „läugnen“ is the 1857 spelling of leugnen (‘to deny’). „ward … genäht … ersäuft“ — the archaic ward forms the passive; ersäufen = to drown (someone). Being sewn into a sack and drowned alive (the Roman ‘poena cullei’) was a real punishment for grave crimes — Grimm does not soften it. „Gebeine des Gemordeten“ = the bones of the murdered man; „zur Ruhe legen“ = to lay to rest.
